messages.content:
The linker error you're encountering is because your `operator>>` is being declared to take a `const Array&` as its second argument, but it is being defined to take a non-const `Array&`. So when the linker is looking for a definition of `operator>>` that matches the declaration, it cannot find one, resulting in the undefined symbol error.

To resolve this issue, change the declaration of `operator>>` in your header file (`3DArray.h`) to match its definition by removing the `const` qualifier:

```cpp
std::istream& operator>>(std::istream& stream, Array& val);
```

The correct signature takes a non-const reference to `Array` because the operator will modify the object `val` by calling `StoreElement` which clearly changes the internal state of the `Array` object.

After making that change, the compiler and linker should be able to find the correct function and link it appropriately, and your program should compile without the linker error.

Remember when you include `"3DArray.h"` in your "main.cpp" file or any other source file, the inclusion acts as if you've pasted the entire content of `3DArray.h` into that file at the point of inclusion. The compiler then needs to ensure that for every declaration in the header file there is a defined function somewhere in the code that is compiled. When this is not the case, you'll get a linker error saying "Undefined symbols" for the referenced function.

Further, make sure you've got the changed declaration and definition of `operator>>` in the correct files that are part of your build. If it's declared in `3DArray.h`, it should be defined in `3DArray.cpp` or directly in `main.cpp` (usually not a good practice - better to separate declaration and definition).

Making `arr_` static within the `Array` class will significantly change the behavior of your class because static members are shared across all instances of the class. This means that every `Array` object you create will share the same `arr_` data. Typically, for a class like `Array`, intended to represent a unique collection of data per instance, having a static array member wouldn't be appropriate.

However, if there is a specific design reason you require `arr_` to be static (which is unusual for an array class), then you need to be aware of the implications:

1. When one instance of `Array` modifies the `arr_`, all other instances see the change because there is only one shared array for all instances.
2. The destructor for `Array` should no longer delete `arr_`, since other instances may still be using it.
3. You'll need a mechanism to manage the lifetime of the `arr_` memory that does not rely on the constructor or destructor of individual `Array` instances.

If you choose to proceed with making `arr_` static, you would change its declaration in the class as follows:

```cpp
private:
    static uint32_t* arr_;
    // ... (rest of the private members)
```

And then you would need to initialize it outside the class definition, in the corresponding `.cpp` file:

```cpp
uint32_t* Array::arr_ = nullptr;
```

The methods of your class that deal with `arr_` now need to ensure that the memory is allocated before it is used and deallocated at the appropriate time. This might involve reference counting or some other memory management strategy.

Given the typical use cases for an array class, I would strongly advise against making `arr_` static unless you have very specific and unusual requirements. If every `Array` is meant to hold its own independent set of data, then `arr_` should definitely not be static.

Please revisit the design intent of your `Array` class and assess whether making `arr_` static actually serves your intended purpose. If this is part of a learning exercise or an interview question that requires a static member for a specific reason, please provide more context so I can give you a more accurate implementation strategy. Otherwise, sticking with a non-static `arr_` for an array-like class is the conventional and recommended approach.
